metadata.title:
STATE INSPECTOR PERLEY
TELLS RAILROAD MEN OF
LEGISLATIVE NEEDS AND
URGES UNITED ACTION THIS
FALL,

pages.raw_text:
The absolute necessity Of the
railroad employees of this state TAX
ing an energetic and intelligent
part in. politics his. fall was. point
ed out by A. w. Perley, state track
and equipment inspector, at meet
ing in Hillyard last evening. He
has addressed trainmen at various
points in the state lately on this
subject and said that he considers
the work of arousing them to
realization of their own interests
one Of the most important functions
of his position.


Two measures were pointed out
specifically as those with which
railroad employees are most vitality
concerned. These are the state
liability law and the limited liabil-
ity law. The former Is designed
to give the railroad employees &C
cess to state courts in prosecutions
for injuries and the second is a
scheme Of the railroads to limit the
amount any railroad mans family
can recover for his death to $4,000.


The state law was defeated in
the senate last session and the lat
ter is expected to come up again
for passage at the next session. An
other effort will also be made to
pass the state liability law, al
though the federal act has been in
validated by the U. s. supreme
court. 1t is proposed to avoid the
technical flaws the supreme court
discovered and enact law that
will stand the test In this state.


To secure this law and defeat the
railroad measure Perley urged the
men to wake up to their RESPONSE
bilities and organize for political
action. He pointed out the vast
improvement made through the le-
gislature in middle and southern
states, where the tracks are level
and but a small percentage of the
danger encountered here exists.
Washington, with every line climb
ing a mountain range, offers train
men the poorest protection of any
state.


The limited liability law, If pass
ed, would make 1t cheaper for rail-
roads to kill employees than to
MAIN them. A man might recover
more than $4,000 for the loss of a
leg but his family could get moth
ing more IL deprived of his support
through fatal accident, and possible
not so much.


Illustrating what had been &C.
complished since the work OF the
railroad commission began IL
months ago, Perley covered in out
line a vast amount of inspection
and rulings resulting in improved
conditions. He declared that he
was in no wise antagonistic to the
companies, and argued that none of
